title: The Velveteen Rabbi's Haggadah for Pesach
creator: Rabbi Rachel Barenblat
summary: A lyrical, contemporary, spiritually open Haggadah with strong beginner preparation, experiential ritual, and source-led transitions; it merits substantial use under its share-with-credit, noncommercial permission, while contemporary conflict poems and embedded works stay separately gated.

sourceAssessment.voice: Poetic, welcoming, contemplative, participatory, and interfaith-accessible, with traditional structure carried by modern spiritual prose and contributed readings.
bestUses: Beginner preparation and a warm explanation of how to participate
A spiritually resonant Maggid and Exodus arc with clear emotional transitions
Plate symbolism, meal rituals, Miriam and Elijah, and a satisfying reflective conclusion
beginnerRisks: Lyrical language sometimes needs a concrete action cue so guests know who does what.
Several pieces are credited to other writers and must keep their specific attribution and rights status.
Some land, Zionist, hostage, and atrocity passages are contemporary or politically incompatible with default generation.
sourceAssessment.politicalContext: The source spans inherited Jerusalem and Israel language, explicit contemporary Zionist and Palestinian discussion, and universal liberation themes. The project can use its enduring spiritual and ritual sequences broadly while applying traditional-only gates to land refrains and current-events gates to conflict material.
sourceAssessment.creditContext: Credit Rabbi Rachel Barenblat and The Velveteen Rabbi's Haggadah for Pesach. The recorded permission permits sharing with credit and prohibits sale; preserve printed credits for all embedded material and do not assume the containing permission clears every third-party work.

credit.readerCredit: From The Velveteen Rabbi's Haggadah for Pesach by Rabbi Rachel Barenblat.
conditions: Share freely and do not sell
Preserve printed credits for embedded material

recommendedSequences.title: Preparing people, objects, and expectations
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Use this before the Seder to explain what guests will read, eat, ask, and do. Add a simple checklist naming the plate, matzah, wine or grape juice, salt water, basin, towels, and optional additions.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The three adjacent preparation records form an unusually strong host-and-guest orientation in the source's own voice, reducing the need for generic AI-written setup.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Rabbi Rachel Barenblat and observe the share-with-credit, do-not-sell condition.

recommendedSequences.title: Entering sacred time together
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Say that the first cup contains wine or grape juice and that the opening marks a change from ordinary dinner to a shared ritual. Explain the first handwashing and offer both self-washing and pouring for one another over a basin.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The opening records create a cohesive threshold from gathering and intention into the first ritual acts, well suited to the source's contemplative voice.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Barenblat and preserve any printed credit attached to the opening poem or blessing.

recommendedSequences.title: Questions that open the story
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Explain that Maggid is the section where the table tells the Exodus story and that questions are part of the ritual. Assign short readings around the table, let guests pass, and give younger participants concrete prompts.
recommendedSequences.rationale: Six consecutive source passages move from ritual questioning into the obligation and permission to retell, preserving the source's invitation to make the Haggadah partly one's own.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Barenblat and retain every source-presented contributor credit in this movement.

recommendedSequences.title: Slavery, courage, and the road out
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Introduce the main figures and state the full arc: a ruler enslaved the Israelites, resistance and courage kept hope alive, Moses confronted Pharaoh, plagues brought suffering, and the people escaped. Pause for compassion before celebrating freedom.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The eight source-order segments preserve a complete modern retelling with narrative momentum, making it a strong source-led backbone rather than a collection of thematic keywords.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Rabbi Rachel Barenblat and all individually credited writers appearing in the selected narrative.

recommendedSequences.title: Plate symbols and an inclusive table
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Point to each named plate object and explain its practical use before its symbolism. If an orange or other optional item appears, say why this table chose it and give an accurate history.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The adjacent records join traditional plate explanation to the source's inclusive additions, letting personalization grow from the ritual object rather than from a detached theme card.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Barenblat and preserve any named originator or quoted source attached to each optional plate practice.

recommendedSequences.title: From handwashing to the Hillel sandwich
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Tell everyone exactly what to do, one action at a time: wash personally or pour for one another, eat matzah, taste bitter herb with haroset, then make the sandwich. Define haroset, explain the blessings, and include allergy substitutions.
recommendedSequences.rationale: Eight consecutive records preserve the correct practical and emotional movement through the symbolic foods, one of the source's clearest ready-to-lead ritual sequences.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Barenblat and preserve printed credits for blessings, translations, poems, or meditations in the sequence.

recommendedSequences.title: Opening the door and filling the cup
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Explain Elijah's cup, Miriam's cup, and who will open the door or pour water. Present hope and welcome as the purpose of the actions and make song or prayer participation optional.
recommendedSequences.rationale: Five adjacent records preserve the source's paired treatment of Elijah and Miriam, balancing inherited hope with women's leadership and the symbolism of sustaining water.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Barenblat and every separately named author or song source appearing in the sequence.

recommendedSequences.title: Carrying freedom beyond the table
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests that Nirtzah closes the formal order but sends the work of freedom into the year ahead. Offer one final shared line and one optional reflection rather than introducing a new discussion.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The four source-order records create a complete contemplative ending, resolving the source's themes instead of stopping after a song or mid-sentence invitation.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Rabbi Rachel Barenblat and observe the noncommercial share-with-credit permission.

reasons: The land and Israel line requires a traditional-only gate and should not enter default generation.
The continuation contains inherited land framing that conflicts with generic peace-focused assembly.
The explicit contemporary Zionist and Palestinian framing requires a deliberate political opt-in and human editorial review.
The contemporary Four Children continuation cannot be presented as evergreen or politically neutral text.
The hostage reference is event-specific and requires current verification.
The contemporary orange poem requires its own rights, date, and context review.
The mass-atrocity language is unsuitable for default use without adult context and current sourcing.
The wrath and atrocity continuation is not eligible for the default peace-focused candidate pool.
The Edward Abbey material requires separate item-level rights and attribution review.
The adapted Michael Walzer material requires its own credit and reuse determination.
The embedded utopian reading remains blocked pending item-level rights review.
The breaking-news poem is date-sensitive and requires independent rights and editorial review.

notes: The owner's permission explicitly supports reuse with attribution and no sale, so the source should be included substantially within those conditions.
A contemplative or interfaith edition can use this source as a coherent backbone by selecting several full movements, not by sampling isolated poetic lines.
Printed contributor credits must survive extraction and final credits even though the project uses one compact source page.
These recommendations are local-agent-reviewed candidates only and do not grant runtime eligibility.
